icpBlurb: US drone maker with defense contracts: ITAR means every design review needs an audit trail, and rapid iteration makes respin costs compound.
snapshot: Designs and manufactures autonomous drones for defense, public safety, and inspection.
Ships US-assembled drone hardware, docks, and autonomy software at scale.

teamSignal.label: Hardware-focused departments hiring (HW, Mfg, Mfg Eng, Supply Chain)

compliance.note: Serves US national security, border security, and military customers with domestically controlled supply chain.

toolchain.sourceQuote: a product stack spanning AI and robotics software, cameras and optics, complex electro-mechanical systems, connectivity, cloud, mobile, aerodynamics, and more
Autonomy Engineer - Deep Learning Infrastructure... Software Engineer - Autonomy Infrastructure, Systems and Tools

hypotheses.title: ITAR audit trail for hardware design changes
hypotheses.evidence: Skydio explicitly serves national security and border security customers under an ITAR-controlled, domestically-built supply chain ('Designed, assembled, and supported in the USA... supply chain meets the highest standards of national security'). Proving who approved every schematic/layout revision and when is a compliance necessity, not a nice-to-have, which is exactly what AllSpice's PR-style sign-offs and audit trails provide.

hypotheses.title: Slow cross-discipline design reviews at scale
hypotheses.evidence: Skydio's product stack spans 'AI and robotics software, cameras and optics, complex electro-mechanical systems, connectivity, cloud, mobile, aerodynamics' with 4 hardware-focused departments hiring simultaneously (Hardware, Manufacturing, Manufacturing Engineering, Supply Chain). Coordinating schematic/layout/BOM review across that many disciplines and open reqs is a natural fit for AllSpice's visual diff and PR-style review workflow.

hypotheses.title: Manufacturing respins risk US-assembly cost/scale
hypotheses.evidence: Skydio has shipped over 60,000 drones with '9 figures of annual revenue with rapid growth' and is actively hiring Manufacturing Engineering roles, meaning any BOM or layout error propagates across a large US-based assembly line. Automation on every revision plus DRCY's grounded review could catch design errors before they trigger costly hardware respins at this production volume.

questions.text: Walk me through how a schematic or layout change gets reviewed today.
How do you coordinate design reviews across electrical, mechanical, and firmware teams?
Who signs off on ECAD changes before they move to manufacturing?
How do you currently prove to auditors who approved a specific revision?
Can you describe a recent respin, what caused it and what it cost?
What catches BOM or layout errors before they reach the assembly line?
How many engineering hours per week go into manual design reviews?
